did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) system for melanoma identification from dermatoscopic images. Specifically, it implements a decision fusion system combining multiple pre-trained CNN architectures (DenseNet 201, VGG 19, MobileNet, and EfficientNet) to classify skin lesions as melanoma or nevus using the ISIC 2019 and PH2 datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and validation of a convolutional neural network system for melanoma identification. It details specific methodologies including the use of ISIC 2019 and PH2 datasets, implementation of a decision fusion system with DenseNet 201, VGG 19, MobileNet, and EfficientNet, and reports new performance metrics (accuracy, F1 score) on these datasets. Although published in a conference proceedings (ICARCV), it contains original results and technical implementation details rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Convolutional Neural Network System for Melanoma Identification
journal: 2024 18th International Conference on Control, Automation, Robotics and Vision (ICARCV)
brief_summary: This study proposes an intelligent system for melanoma identification from dermatoscopic images by fusing the decisions of multiple pre-trained convolutional neural networks. The researchers implemented a decision fusion system combining DenseNet 201, VGG 19, MobileNet, and EfficientNet architectures to classify skin lesions as melanoma or nevus. The model was trained and validated using the ISIC 2019 dataset, which included 5400 balanced images of melanoma and nevus classes. Performance was evaluated on both the ISIC 2019 test set and the independent PH2 dataset to assess robustness across different data origins. The proposed ensemble system achieved an accuracy of 93.33% on ISIC 2019 and 86.50% on PH2, outperforming individual networks and other literature methods. The results demonstrate that decision fusion significantly improves detection performance compared to single-model approaches.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary target class for detection. Used in ISIC 2019 (subset of HAM10000) and PH2 datasets. In PH2, there were 40 images of melanoma.

Comparator class to melanoma. In ISIC 2019, images from the nevus class were selected to balance with melanoma (total 5400 images for both classes). In PH2, 'common nevus' and 'atypical nevus' were grouped into 'pigment nevi' (160 images) because the study did not differentiate between types of nevi.
Mentioned in the context of the PAD-UFES-20 dataset (cited in related work [9]) which contains nevi, melanoma, and serborean keratosis. Not used in the experiments of this paper.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into melanoma or nevus classes.
all_task_types.details: The paper implements a decision fusion system using multiple pre-trained CNNs (DenseNet 201, VGG 19, MobileNet, and EfficientNet) to classify dermatoscopic images as either melanoma or nevus. The task is explicitly described as binary classification, with outputs being probabilities in the interval [0, 1] used for decision-making against a threshold. The primary target classes are melanoma and nevus, using the ISIC 2019 and PH2 datasets.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019 (HAM10000 subset)
datasets_used.description: A public dataset from the ISIC archive, specifically the HAM10000 collection. The study selected only images from the melanoma and nevus classes to create a balanced binary classification dataset.

use.training: Used for training the individual neural networks (DenseNet 201, VGG 19, MobileNet, EfficientNet) and for validation to choose the best networks and determine fusion weights.
use.validation: Used to select the best networks and determine the weights for the decision fusion system.
use.testing: Used to test the decision fusion system and evaluate performance (accuracy 93.33%).

datasets_used.name: PH2
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopic image database available online, used for external testing to evaluate the robustness of the system on images from a different origin.

use.testing: Used for external testing to analyze performance dependence on dataset origin. The images were not used for training.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were based on accuracy and F1 scores without statistical significance testing.

ISIC 2019 (Validation Set).metric: Accuracy (%) and F1 Score (%) for individual networks and fusion combinations
ISIC 2019 (Validation Set).notes: The optimal system consisted of 4 networks (DenseNet121, EfficientNet, MobileNet, and VGG19). The decision threshold was set to 1.73. Individual network accuracies ranged from 85.09% to 91.20%. Fusion systems generally outperformed individual networks.

ISIC 2019 (Test Set).notes: Table VI reports 93% accuracy for the ensemble system on ISIC2019. This aligns with the validation set optimal result of 93.33%.

PH2.notes: The system was tested on the PH2 dataset (200 images: 40 melanoma, 160 pigment nevi) to evaluate robustness on a different origin. Table VI reports 86% accuracy. The abstract mentions 86.50% accuracy.

architecture_used.main_model: Decision fusion ensemble system combining multiple pre-trained Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs). The optimal final system consisted of four networks: DenseNet121, EfficientNet-B7, MobileNet, and VGG19.
architecture_used.pretraining: All individual networks were pre-trained on the ImageNet dataset using transfer learning. Weights were frozen during the specific melanoma detection training phase.
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported in pixels; however, preprocessing ensured uniform resolution for all input images. Specific dimensions are not provided.

architecture_details.modifications: For binary classification, all layers after the last convolutional layer were removed from the pre-trained networks. A MaxPooling image downscaling layer and a flattening layer were added. The original network parameters were frozen during training.
architecture_details.fusion_method: Decision fusion system where the final score (S) for an image is a scalar product of the probability vectors [p] from each network and their corresponding weights [w] (derived from validation F1 scores).
architecture_details.decision_threshold: 1.73 (half of the maximum possible score). Prediction is melanoma if S >= 1.73.

training_details.optimizer: Not explicitly reported (Keras API in TensorFlow 2.14 used).
training_details.loss_function: Modified binary cross-entropy. A factor of 1.3 was applied to the melanoma class (positive criterion) to penalize false negatives more heavily. Formula: L([y], [p]) = - (1/N) * sum(1.3 * y_i * log(p_i) + (1 - y_i) * log(1 - p_i)).
training_details.hardware: 8th generation i5 processor with 4 cores and 8 GB of memory.
training_details.training_time_per_network: Between 3h30min and 4h10min depending on the network.

classification_or_output_head.output: Probability values in the interval [0, 1] for each network, aggregated via weighted sum for the ensemble decision.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Decision fusion ensemble system (combining DenseNet 121, EfficientNet-B7, MobileNet, and VGG 19)
VGG neural network (mentioned in related work)
ResNet50 with attention mechanism (mentioned in related work)
IRv2 with attention mechanism (mentioned in related work)
Hybrid system (DenseNet121 and residual network) (mentioned in related work)
preprocessing_techniques: Selection of specific classes (melanoma and nevus) from the HAM10000 dataset within ISIC 2019 to create a balanced binary classification dataset.
Grouping of 'common nevus' and 'atypical nevus' classes into 'pigment nevi' for the PH2 dataset.
Freezing of original network parameters during the training phase (transfer learning with frozen weights).
Removal of layers after the last convolutional layer from pre-trained networks and addition of MaxPooling and flattening layers for binary classification.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Image sets of equal size were selected for the melanoma and nevus classes from the HAM10000 dataset within ISIC 2019. A total of 5400 dermatoscopic images were used, ensuring balanced classes for training and validation.

Data augmentation was carried out to balance the number of learning images from the two classes. The paper notes that while the selected 5400 images are fewer than other datasets, transfer learning provided good results, implying augmentation was used to mitigate the limited sample size per class.

conclusions: The proposed decision fusion system, combining DenseNet 121, EfficientNet-B7, MobileNet, and VGG 19, achieved an accuracy of 93.33% and an F1 score of 93.30% on the ISIC 2019 validation set, outperforming individual networks and other literature methods.
The ensemble system demonstrated robustness on the independent PH2 dataset, achieving an accuracy of 86.50%, confirming its effectiveness on dermatoscopic images of different origins.
A group of weaker individual networks provided better detection performance through aggregation than a single best-performing network (such as ResNet50), justifying the use of decision fusion.
The system was designed to minimize false negative errors by modifying the cost function with a factor of 1.3 to penalize misclassification of melanoma cases more heavily.
The study acknowledges that some images are incorrectly classified by all networks due to overlapping classes, indicating a limitation in distinguishing between melanoma and pigment nevi.
Transfer learning with frozen weights allowed for efficient training times (between 3h30min and 4h10min per network) while achieving high performance.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a binary classification task (melanoma vs. nevus), excluding other critical skin lesions such as seborrheic keratosis, which limits its clinical applicability for general dermatological screening.

The dataset selection involved manually balancing the ISIC 2019 (HAM10000 subset) by selecting equal numbers of melanoma and nevus images (5400 total), which does not reflect the natural class imbalance found in real-world clinical populations.

The PH2 dataset used for external validation is small (200 images) and has a different class distribution (40 melanoma, 160 pigment nevi) compared to the training data, potentially limiting the robustness assessment.

The paper does not report any statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) for the performance comparisons between the proposed ensemble system and individual networks or literature methods.

The decision threshold for the fusion system was set experimentally to 1.73 (half the maximum score) rather than being optimized via a separate validation protocol or derived from clinical cost-benefit analysis.

The authors acknowledge that some images are incorrectly classified by all networks due to overlapping features between melanoma and pigment nevi, indicating a fundamental limitation in distinguishing these specific classes.

The system was trained on limited hardware (8th gen i5, 8GB RAM), which may not represent the computational requirements for deploying such an ensemble system in resource-constrained clinical environments.

The loss function was modified with a factor of 1.3 to penalize false negatives, but the specific impact of this hyperparameter on the final trade-off between sensitivity and specificity is not deeply analyzed beyond the reported metrics.
